islas-secas-island-microgrid.summary: Islas Secas is an off-grid resort microgrid in Panama's Gulf of Chiriquí built around a 355 kW PV array, advanced lead batteries, SMA inverters, and two 200 kW diesel backup generators. The battery system began with 18 East Penn stacks and was later augmented by nine GS Yuasa stacks under a Nuvation BMS/energy controller. The most detailed battery case study reports 2.1 MWh and 162 kW after augmentation. This is a solar-dominant hybrid system, not literally diesel-free: the resort now markets 100% of property electricity as solar-generated, while the engineering record retains diesel standby and the original design estimate was 90% solar/battery energy.
islas-secas-island-microgrid.status: Operating as the resort's island power system. Islas Secas' 2025 factsheet continues to state that 100% of property electricity is generated by renewable solar, and current Nuvation and Consortium for Battery Innovation pages continue to feature the installation. No audited recent generation, diesel-run-hour, fuel-use, battery-availability, or outage dataset was found, so the current 100% statement is treated as a resort claim about electricity generation rather than proof that backup generators never run.

organizations.name: Islas Secas
organizations.role: Resort owner/operator and microgrid host; publishes the current sustainability claim.

organizations.name: CivicSolar
organizations.role: System designer/integrator for the original hybrid solar project and storage augmentation.

organizations.name: Nuvation Energy
organizations.role: Supplied replacement battery-management systems, the multi-bank ESS controller, and controls for the later storage augmentation.

organizations.name: SMA Solar Technology
organizations.role: Supplied Sunny Tripower PV inverters and Sunny Island battery inverters.

organizations.name: East Penn Manufacturing and GS Yuasa
organizations.role: Supplied the original Deka Unigy II and augmentation SRL 1000 advanced-lead battery stacks, respectively.

specifications.label: Solar PV
specifications.value: 355 kW published system rating, with estimated production of 500,000 kWh/year in SMA's 2019 case account

specifications.label: PV modules
specifications.value: 1,400 Jinko 270 W polycrystalline 60-cell modules; their 378 kWp arithmetic nameplate exceeds the separately published 355 kW system rating, likely reflecting a DC-versus-system-rating convention that the sources do not explain

specifications.label: Storage after augmentation
specifications.value: 2.1 MWh and 162 kW in the Consortium for Battery Innovation technical summary; Nuvation separately describes an existing 1.5 MWh system plus an additional 500 kWh, so public rounded totals differ by about 0.1 MWh

specifications.label: Battery architecture
specifications.value: 27 nominal-48 V lead-battery banks/stacks: 18 East Penn Deka Unigy II stacks plus nine GS Yuasa SRL 1000 stacks

specifications.label: Backup generation
specifications.value: Two 200 kW diesel generator sets, 400 kW aggregate

specifications.label: Inverter count
specifications.value: 18 SMA Sunny Tripower solar inverters and 36 SMA Sunny Island battery inverters

specifications.label: Renewable-energy claim
specifications.value: Original engineering publicity estimated PV plus batteries would supply 90% of energy; the resort's 2025 factsheet says 100% of property electricity is generated by solar. Neither source publishes the accounting interval or diesel contribution behind the later claim.

equipment.detail: 1,400 modules installed beside the island airstrip.
Eighteen PV inverters and 36 bidirectional battery inverters form the AC-coupled system.
Advanced lead batteries of different manufacturers, capacities, ages, and states of health are operated as one system by configurable Nuvation controls.
Nuvation BMS units replaced the original BMS on the East Penn banks and were added to the GS Yuasa banks; a custom ESS controller coordinates all 27 banks and two diesel gensets.

technicalDetails.detail: PV serves island loads and charges storage during the day; at dusk, the resort normally transfers to silent battery operation. Diesel sets are available for heavy loads or prolonged cloudy periods.

The augmentation did not discard the original 18 stacks. Nuvation replaced their BMS, added nine new stacks, and programmed the EMS to prioritize cycling across banks with different charge/discharge profiles and states of health.

Use 355 kW as the repeatedly published plant rating while retaining 378 kWp as module arithmetic. For storage, preserve the 1.5 MWh original, approximately 0.5 MWh augmentation, and 2.1 MWh technical-summary values instead of forcing a false exact total.
No reviewed source discloses commissioning date, hourly operating data, recent diesel use, battery replacement history after the GS Yuasa augmentation, or measured outage/reliability performance.

sources.title: Solar Energy Storage for Island
Solar Spotlight – Islas Secas: Unmatched Sustainable Tourism Model in Panama
Nuvation Energy – Solar powered eco-resort in Panama
Islas Secas General Factsheet

jo-island.summary: This record is the proposed Jodo (조도) Energy-Independent Island project in Jodo-myeon, Jindo-gun, South Jeolla—not an operating project called simply 'Jo Island.' The LG CNS consortium's plan paired 294 kW of PV, two 750 kW wind turbines, an 8.12 MWh ESS with 4.25 MW PCS, and the existing 3.6 MW oil-fired internal-combustion plant. It targeted 45% renewable supply. Evidence from 2018 and a 2022 Korea Energy Economics Institute study shows that Jodo and the other five-island private-investment projects did not reach a PPA/SPC implementation stage; the planned renewable assets should not be counted as installed.
jo-island.status: Stalled/unbuilt proposal. LG CNS still described Jodo as a selected track record in its 2016-2018 sustainability materials, but the 2022 academic review found that none of the five follow-on islands had signed a PPA and a December 2018 Korean report said the same five had failed to establish project companies or show implementation progress after tariff-policy changes destroyed the expected economics. No later authoritative evidence of construction or commissioning at Jodo was located.

organizations.name: LG CNS consortium
organizations.role: Selected private consortium/project organizer for the proposed Jodo and Geomundo energy-independent island projects; intended microgrid/EPC and control-system provider.

organizations.name: Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEPCO)
organizations.role: Incumbent island electricity supplier and intended 20-year power purchaser; Jodo remained served by its existing internal-combustion plant.

organizations.name: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE)
organizations.role: National program sponsor and rulemaker for the private-investment Energy-Independent Island initiative.

organizations.name: Jindo-gun / Jodo community
organizations.role: Proposed host jurisdiction and population/load served by the existing island system.

specifications.label: Planned solar PV
specifications.value: 294 kW; proposed, not verified as installed

specifications.label: Planned wind
specifications.value: 1.5 MW total from two 750 kW turbines; proposed, not verified as installed

specifications.label: Planned energy storage
specifications.value: 8.12 MWh ESS with 4.25 MW power-conversion system; chemistry, supplier, and duration were not specified in the reviewed plan

specifications.label: Existing generation
specifications.value: 3.6 MW internal-combustion plant: two 500 kW units and one 2.6 MW unit

specifications.label: Target renewable share
specifications.value: 45% of island electricity demand

specifications.label: 2015 island electricity baseline
specifications.value: 8,300 MWh generation, approximately $399/MWh generating cost, population 2,351 in 905 households, and 35.7 km² area

specifications.label: Indicative investment
specifications.value: $43 million in the 2016 plan table

equipment.detail: Two 750 kW wind turbines and 294 kW of PV were specified only at aggregate level; no manufacturer or model is identified, consistent with a proposal that had not reached procurement.

The plan specified 8.12 MWh of ESS and 4.25 MW PCS and assigned the project to LG CNS, whose offering included an energy management/microgrid control system. No evidence shows this equipment was ordered or installed at Jodo.
Three oil-fired internal-combustion units totaling 3.6 MW remained the documented supply base; engine manufacturers and fuel subtype are not disclosed in the reviewed sources.

technicalDetails.detail: Private operators were expected to finance renewables, ESS, and EMS, then recover investment through a 20-year electricity sale to KEPCO. The project was therefore contingent on a bankable PPA rather than a normal government-funded demonstration procurement.

Falling oil prices and a 2016 change from a fixed island renewable price to compensation linked to diesel operating cost substantially reduced expected revenue. The five selected follow-on islands, including Jodo, consequently failed to establish SPCs or conclude PPAs.

Use Jodo, Jodo-myeon, Jindo-gun, Jeollanam-do as the project identity. There are multiple Korean islands named Jodo; this proposal is not the much smaller Jodo near Namhae and not the Jo Island spelling used in the recovered record.

All renewable and storage figures are planned capacity. Only the 3.6 MW conventional plant and 2015 generation baseline are documented as existing; representing 1.794 MW of renewables or 8.12 MWh as operational would materially overstate deployment.

sources.title: A case study on the implementation environment of Korea's energy-independent island projects
sources.publisher: Korea Energy Economics Institute / International Journal of Smart Grid and Clean Energy

kalkeri-sangeet-vidyalaya-dc-microgrid.summary: Kalkeri Sangeet Vidyalaya's campus energy project near Dharwad, Karnataka was built with its $100,000 2014 Zayed Future Energy Prize award and SELCO India/SELCO Foundation support. The installed package included a 14 kW AC solar mini-grid for classrooms, hostels, office, hall, and kitchen; a separate 5 kW solar water-pumping system; solar hot water; biogas; LEDs; and daylighting improvements. The recovered title calls it a DC microgrid, but the school's own annual report says AC mini-grid and the detailed configuration has a one-phase AC output. Its 120 × 2 V × 300 Ah battery inventory equals 72 kWh nominal, not the recovered 17 kWh.
kalkeri-sangeet-vidyalaya-dc-microgrid.status: Installed in 2014-2015 and historically described as operational. KSV remains an active school with 230 students in 2023-2024 and still lists SELCO Foundation as a partner; a 2024 partner update says local electricity rationing continues to affect the school. No recent technical report confirms present battery health, solar output, outage coverage, or whether the original lead-acid bank has been replaced, so current microgrid availability is not independently verified.

organizations.name: Kalkeri Sangeet Vidyalaya (KSV)
organizations.role: School, project owner, beneficiary, and 2014 Zayed Future Energy Prize recipient.

organizations.name: SELCO India / SELCO Foundation
organizations.role: Energy auditor, designer, installer, and implementation partner for the mini-grid, solar pump, and water-heating systems.

organizations.name: Zayed Future Energy Prize (now Zayed Sustainability Prize)
organizations.role: Awarded KSV the 2014 Global High Schools Asia prize and $100,000 implementation grant.

organizations.name: Kumar LaNoce Architects
organizations.role: Partner named by KSV for the broader campus sustainability project.

specifications.label: Campus solar mini-grid
specifications.value: 14 kWp from 50 × 280 Wp panels in ten-series by five-parallel configuration

specifications.label: Battery bank
specifications.value: 120 × 2 V, 300 Ah batteries on two racks; 72 kWh nominal arithmetic capacity. The recovered 17 kWh value is inconsistent with the disclosed cell inventory.

specifications.label: Electrical interface
specifications.value: Three-phase grid input and single-phase AC output distributed through a four-core underground cable to building clusters

specifications.label: Separate solar pumping
specifications.value: 5 kW solar system for the campus bore-well pump

specifications.label: Thermal and efficiency measures
specifications.value: Five solar water-heating systems, a kitchen biogas system, campus-wide LED conversion, improved classroom daylighting, and LED classroom projectors

specifications.label: Loads served
specifications.value: Classrooms, student hostels, main hall/Yogashala, office, kitchen, lights, fans, televisions, laptops, digital-education equipment, and washing machine

equipment.detail: Fifty 280 Wp modules mounted on a custom module-mounting structure above the battery room; manufacturer and model are not disclosed.
A 120-cell 2 V/300 Ah bank and a power-conditioning unit serve the mini-grid. Battery chemistry, PCU manufacturer/model, and usable depth of discharge are not public.
Building clusters connect through distribution boxes with MCB protection. Each distribution point has an energy meter and consumption data is logged to a central server for load auditing and control.

technicalDetails.detail: Despite the recovered record's 'DC microgrid' title, KSV's annual report explicitly calls the installation a 14 kW AC solar mini-grid, while the technical description specifies one-phase AC output. The DC battery/PV subsystem does not make the campus distribution network a DC microgrid.

The electrical mini-grid was one part of a student-led sustainability package that also reduced load through efficient lighting and daylighting and displaced other energy uses through solar hot water, solar pumping, and biogas.

Submetering was designed to expose excess consumption by building cluster and allow school staff to intervene. The reviewed sources do not provide logged generation/consumption data or measured savings from the planned before-and-after energy audits.

KSV and its partner network remain active, but a 2024 funding story still cites electricity rationing at Kalkeri. That does not prove failure of the mini-grid—loads may have grown or storage may be aging—but it prevents claiming full energy independence without a current engineering report.

sources.title: Kalkeri Sangeet Vidyalaya Annual Report 2014-15
Zayed Future Energy Prize Winner Partners With Finalist to Implement Winning Project
sources.publisher: Zayed Future Energy Prize / Business Wire

Who we are – Kalkeri Sangeet Vidyalaya
Sustainable energy and a connected future at the Kalkeri Sangeet Vidyalaya School in India

kema.summary: The recovered 'KEMA' project is Leonics' abbreviated label for the RPS Kemar solar-diesel-battery mini-grid in Gerik, Perak, Malaysia—not Kemayan, Pahang. Supplier and TNB presentations document an 850 kWp PV array split between 600 kWp DC-coupled and 250 kWp AC-coupled sections, 850 kW of inverter capacity, 4.8 MWh of 480 VDC battery storage, and four diesel generators totaling 1.6 MW. The project's published 3.30 MW label is Leonics' sum of PV, inverter, and diesel ratings, not a conventional net generation-capacity figure.
kema.status: Commissioned in November 2012 for 24-hour rural electrification. A 2018 TNB Energy Services company profile still listed TNBES as EPC and O&M contractor for 342 consumers, but no later project-specific operating report was located. The plant should be described as commissioned/historically operating, with present battery condition, diesel consumption, renewable share, and service availability unverified.

organizations.name: Ministry of Rural and Regional Development Malaysia
organizations.role: Project owner under the Bekalan Elektrik Luar Bandar rural-electrification program.

organizations.name: TNB Energy Services Sdn. Bhd. (TNBES)
organizations.role: Turnkey/EPC contractor and later listed operation-and-maintenance provider.

organizations.name: Leonics Co., Ltd.
organizations.role: Hybrid power-conversion, charge-control, system-control, and monitoring equipment supplier/integrator.

organizations.name: RPS Kemar Orang Asli communities
organizations.role: End users across remote settlements, including homes, school, clinic, mosque, and government facilities.

specifications.label: Solar PV
specifications.value: 850 kWp total: 600 kWp DC-coupled and 250 kWp AC-coupled

specifications.label: Battery storage
specifications.value: 4,800 kWh nominal at 480 VDC; chemistry and usable energy are not disclosed

specifications.label: Diesel generation
specifications.value: 1,600 kW total: two 450 kW and two 350 kW generator sets

specifications.label: Inverter and charge-control capacity
specifications.value: Three 200 kW bidirectional battery inverters (600 kW), one 250 kW grid-connected inverter, and nine 70 kW MPPT charge controllers (630 kW controller aggregate)

specifications.label: Published system label
specifications.value: 3.30 MW, defined by Leonics as total power of inverter + diesel generator + PV (850 + 1,600 + 850 kW). It is not an additive generation nameplate because inverter capacity is counted alongside the resources it converts.

specifications.label: Beneficiaries
specifications.value: TNB's 2015 presentation says approximately 5,000 residents; TNBES' 2018 profile says 342 consumers including houses, mosque, government office, clinic, and schools. These are different population/customer metrics.

specifications.label: Schedule
specifications.value: Contractor start October 2010; commissioning/completion November 2012

equipment.detail: Three 200 kW units provide 600 kW of grid-forming/bidirectional conversion. The reviewed project diagram does not give the commercial model.
A 250 kW grid-connected inverter serves the AC-coupled PV section; nine 70 kW MPPT charge controllers serve the 600 kWp DC-coupled section.
The supplier architecture uses a System Monitoring Unit, Inverter System Controller, and Hybrid System Control and Command Unit for coordinated PV, battery, and diesel dispatch and remote monitoring.
Aggregate ratings and diesel-unit sizes are public, but battery manufacturer/chemistry and genset manufacturers/models are not identified in the reviewed authoritative material.

technicalDetails.detail: Daytime loads can be served by 250 kWp of AC-coupled PV while the 600 kWp DC-coupled array charges the 480 V battery through MPPT controllers. Battery inverters and diesel sets support the common isolated AC mini-grid and provide overnight/low-solar supply.

Leonics alternates between 'Kema' in overview graphics and 'Kemar Village, Perak' in the detailed diagram. TNB names the site RPS Kemar, Grik/Gerik, Perak. The recovered coordinates near Kemayan, Pahang are therefore a geocoding error and should not be used for this asset.
The system was intended to provide expandable 24-hour electricity with remote monitoring to settlements and public facilities that lacked normal grid service.

No project-specific public source reviewed after the 2018 O&M listing reports energy production, PV share, diesel savings, outages, battery replacements, or whether the original 4.8 MWh bank remains in service.

sources.title: MW scale Stand-Alone Hybrid Mini-Grid System
Inverter Technologies in Isolated Microgrid
Renewable Energy: TNB Experiences Towards Rural Electrification
TNB Energy Services Company Profile 2018

kiffa-hybrid-project.summary: Kiffa is an operating 6.3 MW solar-thermal plant about 13 km from Kiffa, Mauritania, supplying Kiffa and Guérou through a 33 kV network. It combines 1.3 MWp of PV with four HFO-capable engine-generators totaling 5 MW. Vergnet delivered the solar portion and ABC Contracting/ABC Engines the thermal portion under SOMELEC/AFD-EU sponsorship. Unlike some generic microgrid listings, it has no documented battery: renewable variability is balanced by dispatchable thermal generation on the isolated regional grid.
kiffa-hybrid-project.status: Operating but capacity-constrained and undergoing maintenance. Commissioning is reported as April 2018, with public inauguration in May 2019. An AFD evaluation found the installed capacity insufficient for demand growth by 2020 because a planned expansion had been delayed. The PV plant produced nearly 2.3 GWh in 2021 with strong measured performance, while SOMELEC's 2025 procurement records show an ABB motor rewinding service and planned replacement of failed generating set No. 1, evidence of continued use but also equipment issues.

organizations.name: Société Mauritanienne d'Électricité (SOMELEC)
organizations.role: Delegated project authority, owner/operator, network utility, and current maintenance procurer.

organizations.name: Ministry of Petroleum, Energy and Mines of Mauritania
organizations.role: Contracting authority for the plant and associated regional electricity infrastructure.

organizations.name: Vergnet
organizations.role: EPC/equipment provider for the 1.3 MWp solar component and hybrid integration partner.

organizations.name: ABC Contracting / Anglo Belgian Corporation
organizations.role: EPC and engine supplier for the 5 MW heavy-fuel-oil thermal component.

organizations.name: Agence Française de Développement and European Union
organizations.role: Financiers for the plant, Kiffa-Guérou line, substations, distribution reinforcement, and household connections.

specifications.label: Total generation
specifications.value: 6.3 MW: 1.3 MWp solar PV plus 5 MW thermal generation

specifications.label: PV array
specifications.value: 4,320 modules, 36 strings, fixed at 15° tilt facing south

specifications.label: PV inverters
specifications.value: Two ABB PVS800-57-630 kW units

specifications.label: Thermal units
specifications.value: Four 750 rpm engines: two 1,000 kW and two 1,500 kW, with four 400 V/50 Hz alternators; HFO is the documented primary fuel and the original design also referenced LFO/HFO capability

specifications.label: Grid and service area
specifications.value: 0.4/33 kV plant interconnection and approximately 60 km Kiffa-Guérou medium-voltage link, serving both towns and settlements along the route

specifications.label: 2021 PV performance
specifications.value: Approximately 2.3 GWh; annual performance ratio 86.33%, capacity factor 19.91%, inverter efficiency 98.31%, final yield 4.78 h/day, and array yield 4.86 h/day

specifications.label: Financing
specifications.value: €23.8 million cited by the Mauritanian legislative account: €19 million AFD loan plus €4.9 million EU grant (rounding causes a €0.1 million arithmetic difference)

equipment.detail: Two central inverters; the study reports 98.31% average inverter efficiency for the 2021 monitoring year.
Four 750 rpm HFO engine-generator sets arranged as two 1 MW and two 1.5 MW units. Commercial engine model numbers are not consistently documented in the reviewed plant-specific sources.
The performance paper identifies 4,320 polycrystalline-silicon modules but the reviewed accessible text does not reliably expose the module manufacturer/model.
A plant command/control system coordinates PV and thermal production on the isolated 33 kV network. No battery energy-storage system is listed by the EPC, AFD, government, or performance sources.

technicalDetails.detail: PV reduces fuel use while the four synchronous thermal units provide dispatchability and grid support. The plant was conceived as the sole central station on Kiffa's isolated grid and later linked Guérou; it is a hybrid power station rather than a battery-centered microgrid.

Analysis of January-December 2021 supervisory data estimated that the PV portion displaced more than 550 tonnes of fossil fuel. Dust and high summer module temperatures produced seasonal capture losses, while annual inverter and system performance remained strong.

AFD judged the project successful but reported that accumulated implementation delays erased the demand-growth margin: an extension expected by 2017-2018 had not occurred, and installed capacity was already insufficient by 2020.

SOMELEC's 2025 procurement plan calls for replacing failed unit No. 1 with a new engine, while a September 2025 notice sought rewinding of an ABB electric motor at the plant. These records support operating relevance but also flag reduced thermal availability pending repair/replacement.

sources.title: Evaluation du projet de construction d'une centrale hybride thermo-photovoltaïque à Kiffa
Performance analysis of a photovoltaic component integrated into a hybrid power plant in Southeast Mauritania
sources.publisher: International Journal of Renewable Energy Development

sources.title: Kiffa Hybrid Power Plant
SOMELEC 2025 investment procurement plan, revision 12

The President of the Republic visits the Kiffa hybrid power plant

kihola-bay-residential-solar-microgrid-h.summary: The legacy 'Kihola Bay' entry is the Bakken Hale Estate microgrid at Kīholo Bay/North Kona, Hawaiʻi. Renewable Energy Services completed the off-grid system in June 2015 for Earl Bakken's 17,000-square-foot estate. Original sources describe approximately 172-176 kW of PV, 1 MWh of 48 V Aquion aqueous-hybrid-ion storage, SMA inverters, MidNite charge controllers, and propane/biodiesel backup. The legacy 1,000 MWh storage value is a three-order-of-magnitude error. RES' current case page now lists Blue Planet Energy Blue Ion HI batteries, indicating a storage retrofit from the original Aquion system, although its date and exact replacement capacity are not disclosed.
kihola-bay-residential-solar-microgrid-h.status: Completed June 2015 and presented by the installer as a successful operating case. The current RES page retains a 176 kW/1 MWh project rating and shows Blue Ion HI batteries plus biodiesel backup. No current operating dataset or retrofit announcement was found, so present battery capacity, annual solar fraction, backup-fuel consumption, and estate operation should be treated as unverified beyond the installer case page.

organizations.name: Bakken Hale Estate / Earl Bakken
organizations.role: Private project owner and host; commissioned the replacement of aging generator-dominated supply.

organizations.name: Renewable Energy Services, Inc. (RES)
organizations.role: System designer, integrator, installer, and current case-study publisher.

organizations.name: Aquion Energy
organizations.role: Original supplier of the 1 MWh saltwater/aqueous-hybrid-ion battery system.

organizations.name: Blue Planet Energy
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the Blue Ion HI battery system now listed by RES, apparently replacing the original Aquion bank.

organizations.name: SMA Solar Technology, MidNite Solar, SunPower, and Tigo Energy
organizations.role: Suppliers of inverters, charge controllers, PV modules, and module-level optimizers/monitoring.

specifications.label: Solar PV
specifications.value: Original Aquion design: 172 kW; current RES case page: 176 kW. RES specifies SunPower 345 W modules, and historical reporting gives 512 modules, whose 176.64 kWp arithmetic explains the rounded 176 kW value.

specifications.label: Original storage
specifications.value: 1 MWh at 48 V using Aquion M-Line aqueous-hybrid-ion modules; not 1,000 MWh

specifications.label: Current storage listing
specifications.value: 1 MWh project rating with Blue Planet Energy Blue Ion HI batteries on RES' current case page; retrofit date, cell count, and usable-energy rating are not published

specifications.label: Load
specifications.value: 23 kW average and 42 kW peak in the original Aquion design account

specifications.label: Autonomy and backup
specifications.value: Designed for three days of autonomous clean-energy operation, with generator backup; original documentation says propane, while the current RES page says biodiesel

specifications.label: Expected energy and fuel outcomes
specifications.value: 350 MWh/year solar production and 97% fossil-fuel reduction were original design/marketing estimates; no recent measured result was found

specifications.label: Site loads
specifications.value: 17,000 ft² residence plus desalination, greenhouse, ballroom, and commercial air conditioning

equipment.detail: 1 MWh, 48 V AHI system using a water-based electrolyte, manganese-oxide cathode, and carbon-composite anode. It is no longer the battery brand listed by the installer.
RES' live case study identifies Blue Ion HI batteries and shows the replacement battery room; no public retrofit date or detailed configuration was located.
SunPower 345 W modules with Tigo module-level power optimizers and cloud monitoring/shutdown capability.

equipment.model: Sunny Island, Sunny Boy, and Classic
equipment.detail: SMA battery and PV inverters work with MidNite Classic charge controllers in the original 48 V off-grid architecture.

technicalDetails.detail: The original battery charged from PV during roughly eight daylight hours and discharged across an approximately 16-hour non-solar interval; standby generation covered multi-day low-solar periods and contingencies.

The coastal estate could not practically be served by utility lines and had relied on three aging propane generators with unusually expensive fuel delivery. Solar and storage were intended to remove noise and reduce fuel dependence.

Original 2015 sources uniformly identify Aquion AHI storage, whereas RES now names Blue Ion HI. The safest interpretation is a later battery retrofit, not two simultaneous 1 MWh banks; absent a dated retrofit source, exact timing and retained Aquion equipment are unknown.
Use Kīholo Bay/Bakken Hale rather than 'Kihola.' Preserve 172 kW as the original engineering label and 176 kW as the installer/module-nameplate value. Correct storage from 1,000 MWh to 1 MWh.

sources.title: Bakken Estate: Cutting-edge microgrid with a 6-year payback
Aquion Energy Sales Introduction – Solar Microgrid, Hawaii, USA
Progress made on NELHA's energy storage initiative
sources.publisher: Natural Energy Laboratory of Hawaii Authority

sources.title: Program on Technology Innovation: Microgrid Implementations – Literature Review

king-island.summary: Hydro Tasmania's King Island system is a continuously evolving off-grid utility network, not a single static project. The 2010-2016 KIREIP program integrated 2.45 MW of wind, 470 kW of solar, a 3 MW/approximately 1.5 MWh advanced lead-acid battery, two 1 MVA diesel-UPS flywheels, a 1.5 MW dynamic resistor, demand response, diesel/biodiesel generation, and autonomous controls. A new 1.5 MW/5,000-panel solar farm entered service in 2023, and Hydro Tasmania began a $11.5 million wind-farm repowering plus battery replacement in September 2024, due for completion in 2027.
king-island.status: Operating and under refurbishment. ARENA marks the original KIREIP funding project complete as of April 19, 2016, while the island power system continues in service. Hydro Tasmania reported that diesel use had fallen 50% over the decade to 2023, saving 2.1 million liters and 5,700 tCO2 annually; the new solar farm was forecast to save another 300,000 liters and 800 tCO2 per year. The five wind turbines and battery are now being refurbished/replaced through 2027.

organizations.name: Hydro Tasmania
organizations.role: Owner/operator, lead developer, generation/distribution service provider, and sponsor of current solar, wind, and battery upgrades.

organizations.name: Australian Renewable Energy Agency (ARENA)
organizations.role: Provided $5.95 million toward the $17.69 million KIREIP demonstration and maintains the completion record.

organizations.name: Tasmanian Government
organizations.role: Original KIREIP project partner and public owner of Hydro Tasmania.

organizations.name: Entura
organizations.role: Hydro Tasmania consulting business that provided electrical/control engineering, smart-grid development, dashboards, and later solar-farm support.

specifications.label: Original wind fleet
specifications.value: 2.45 MW total: three 250 kW turbines installed in 1998 and two 850 kW Vestas V52 turbines installed in 2003

specifications.label: KIREIP-era solar
specifications.value: 470 kW published by Hydro/ARENA-era sources; some engineering case material isolates a 95 kW utility array, so 470 kW likely includes multiple island PV installations and should not be conflated with a single array

specifications.label: New solar farm
specifications.value: 1.5 MW, 5,000 panels, almost six hectares, completed in 2023 at a cost of $3.35 million

specifications.label: KIREIP battery
specifications.value: 3 MW/1.5 MWh advanced lead-acid storage in Entura's project account; other sources cite 1.6 MWh usable, reflecting a reporting/usable-energy difference

specifications.label: Grid-stability equipment
specifications.value: Two 1 MVA flywheel-based diesel UPS units, 1.5 MW dynamic resistor, sub-second aggregated demand response, smart metering/switches, and advanced hybrid controls

specifications.label: Conventional plant and load
specifications.value: 6 MW diesel station, approximately 12 GWh annual demand, and 2.5 MW peak demand in Hydro Tasmania's system overview

specifications.label: Renewable performance
specifications.value: KIREIP was designed for 65% annual renewable energy and 100% instantaneous renewable operation. APEC's 2023 review says the system operates diesel-off about 20% of the year and reduced annual diesel use from 4.5 to 2.6 million liters.

equipment.manufacturer: Vestas and original 250 kW turbine supplier not consistently named in current primary sources
equipment.detail: All five turbines are being repowered using refurbished nacelles and restored foundations, towers, and blades; the work began in September 2024 and is due in 2027.

The KIREIP system used an advanced lead-acid battery rated about 3 MW/1.5-1.6 MWh. Hydro Tasmania's 2024 repowering announcement confirms a new battery is included but does not publish its chemistry, supplier, power, or energy rating.
Two large rotating flywheels provide inertia and bridge sudden renewable shortfalls while diesel engines start only when extended support is required.

Hydro Tasmania's hybrid control system coordinates generation, battery, flywheels, resistor, and demand-side devices fast enough to move autonomously between diesel-on and extended diesel-off operation.

technicalDetails.detail: Battery and rotating flywheel systems supply frequency/voltage support and fault response when conventional generators are off. The dynamic resistor absorbs sudden excess renewable power, and sub-second demand response helps rebalance the isolated grid.

The original diesel-only system consumed about 4.5 million liters/year. APEC reports roughly 2.6 million liters after KIREIP, while Hydro's 2024 annual report expresses the decade result as 2.1 million liters and 5,700 tCO2 saved annually. These are compatible baselines/rounding, not exact live telemetry.

The 2023 solar farm and 2024-2027 wind/battery work post-date the original KIREIP inventory. Current total solar cannot safely be calculated by simply adding 470 kW and 1.5 MW without confirmation that all earlier arrays remain in service.

Earlier dual-axis PV and concentrated-solar trials were abandoned, and a 2003 vanadium-redox-flow battery failed and was decommissioned before the advanced lead-acid KIREIP battery. Those superseded technologies should not appear in a current equipment total.

Hydro Tasmania is reusing the five wind-turbine towers, foundations, and blades where possible and installing restored nacelles refurbished in Denmark. The $11.5 million program is intended to extend wind-farm life by at least ten years and includes battery replacement.

kings-contrivance-village-center.summary: Kings Contrivance Village Center in Columbia, Maryland was one of two public-purpose microgrids proposed by Baltimore Gas and Electric in December 2015. The Kings Contrivance site would have used 2 MW of natural-gas generation, islanded during major distribution outages, to serve a cluster of grocery, pharmacy, fuel, restaurant, bank, and other critical commercial services. The Maryland Public Service Commission denied the two-site proposal without prejudice on July 19, 2016. It was never an operating 3 MW microgrid, and it included no solar or battery storage.
kings-contrivance-village-center.status: Denied and not built. Maryland PSC Order 87669 rejected the proposal and Rider 12 tariff while allowing BGE to submit a redesigned project later. The reviewed PSC record and subsequent NREL regulatory case summary show no approval for this site; no later BGE or PSC evidence of a Kings Contrivance resubmission, construction, or operation was located.

organizations.name: Baltimore Gas and Electric Company (BGE)
organizations.role: Proposed developer, owner, operator, generator owner, and tariff applicant.

organizations.name: Maryland Public Service Commission
organizations.role: State regulator; denied the proposal as filed without prejudice and rejected the proposed Rider 12 surcharge.

organizations.name: Howard County
organizations.role: Host county; conditionally supported the concept subject to siting approval, public participation, and timely removal after the pilot.

organizations.name: Kings Contrivance Community Association and Village Center merchants
organizations.role: Community stakeholder and intended host/beneficiaries of resilient commercial service during outages; merchants were not asked to co-invest in the filed plan.

specifications.label: Proposed Kings Contrivance generation
specifications.value: 2 MW natural-gas-fired generation; diesel was the generic alternative if gas infrastructure proved unavailable

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Natural gas only in the Kings Contrivance design; no renewable generation, CHP, or energy storage was proposed

specifications.label: Estimated Kings Contrivance cost
specifications.value: $7 million, based on BGE's estimate of approximately $3.5 million per MW of supported load

specifications.label: Two-site pilot cost
specifications.value: Approximately $16.6 million for Kings Contrivance plus the separate 3 MW Edmonson Village site

specifications.label: Proposed residential surcharge
specifications.value: $0.04/month in year one and $0.13/month in year two for a 930 kWh/month average residential customer, continuing while assets depreciated if no wider rollout occurred

specifications.label: Potential market revenue
specifications.value: BGE estimated roughly $215,000/year per generation unit from participation in PJM energy, capacity, ancillary-service, or demand-response markets

equipment.detail: Two megawatts of small-scale natural-gas generation were specified functionally, but generator count, manufacturer, model, heat rate, emissions controls, and black-start equipment were not identified in the PSC order.

The design would disconnect the commercial-service footprint from BGE's distribution system during an extended outage and energize selected critical businesses. Control/protection vendors and switching topology were not disclosed.

None proposed. The Commission specifically criticized the absence of renewable generation, combined heat and power, and storage as a lost opportunity for fuel diversity and forward-looking demonstration value.

technicalDetails.detail: The microgrid was intended to keep grocery, pharmacy, fuel, restaurant, bank, and similar commercial services open during significant or extended outages, potentially serving residents who could travel to the village center.

BGE had not quantified customer-interruption/resilience benefits, conducted a cost-benefit analysis, justified socializing all risk through a surcharge, pursued grants or beneficiary investment, defined a major event, or shown that customers could reach and resupply the site during disasters.

BGE selected the two sites without initial customer, county, or state-agency participation, relied on 2010-2012 SAIFI/SAIDI data, did not explain factor weighting, and did not establish a competitive third-party generation process.

The 2-3 MW language in the order describes the range across two proposed sites: Kings Contrivance was 2 MW and Edmonson Village was 3 MW. The recovered 3 MW value for Kings Contrivance is therefore incorrect.

Denial was without prejudice, meaning BGE could file a better-supported pilot later; it was not an authorization to construct. Absent a later approved filing, the correct project status remains denied/unbuilt rather than planned or operational.

sources.title: Order No. 87669, Case No. 9416 – BGE Microgrid Order
